title: Alert Severity Trending
spl:
index=ids sourcetype="snort:alert"
| timechart span=1h count by priority
cimSpl:
| tstats `summariesonly` count
  from datamodel=Intrusion_Detection.IDS_Attacks
  by IDS_Attacks.severity IDS_Attacks.signature IDS_Attacks.src span=1h
| sort -count
dataModelAcceleration: Enable Data Model Acceleration for CIM Intrusion_Detection, dataset IDS_Attacks, after Snort, Suricata, pan:threat, or vendor IDS/IPS logs are tagged and field-aliased into the model; the `cimSpl` tstats search requires a healthy accelerated summary.
description: Trending IDS alerts reveals attack patterns, campaign surges, and tuning opportunities. Supports SOC workload planning.
value: Trending IDS alerts reveals attack patterns, campaign surges, and tuning opportunities. Supports SOC workload planning.
grandmaExplanation: We track how many network-threat alerts fire at each severity over time, so we can spot surges, noisy rules, and attack campaigns that need analyst attention or tuning.
implementation: Forward IDS alerts to Splunk via syslog. Normalize severity/priority fields. Track alert volume by severity over time. Identify noisy signatures for tuning. Alert on sudden spikes in high-severity events.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: TA-suricata, Cisco Secure Firewall syslog.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: IDS/IPS alert logs.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Forward IDS alerts to Splunk via syslog. Normalize severity/priority fields. Track alert volume by severity over time. Identify noisy signatures for tuning. Alert on sudden spikes in high-severity events.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=ids sourcetype="snort:alert"
| timechart span=1h count by priority
```

Understanding this SPL

**Alert Severity Trending** — Trending IDS alerts reveals attack patterns, campaign surges, and tuning opportunities. Supports SOC workload planning.

Documented **Data sources**: IDS/IPS alert logs. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): TA-suricata, Cisco Secure Firewall syslog. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: ids; **sourcetype**: snort:alert. If that sourcetype is not mentioned in Data sources, double-check parsing or update the documentation to match the feed you actually ingest.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=ids, sourcetype="snort:alert". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `timechart` plots the metric over time using **span=1h** buckets with a separate series **by priority** — ideal for trending and alerting on this use case.

Optional CIM / accelerated variant (same use case, normalized fields via Common Information Model):

```spl
| tstats `summariesonly` count
  from datamodel=Intrusion_Detection.IDS_Attacks
  by IDS_Attacks.src IDS_Attacks.signature IDS_Attacks.severity span=1h
| sort -count
```

Understanding this CIM / accelerated SPL

**Alert Severity Trending** — Trending IDS alerts reveals attack patterns, campaign surges, and tuning opportunities. Supports SOC workload planning.

Documented **Data sources**: IDS/IPS alert logs. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): TA-suricata, Cisco Secure Firewall syslog. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

This **CIM or accelerated** block uses normalized field names and/or `tstats` over data models. Enable **acceleration** on the referenced models (and correct CIM knowledge objects) or the search may return nothing.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Uses `tstats` against accelerated summaries for data model `Intrusion_Detection.IDS_Attacks` — enable acceleration for that model.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Enable Data Model Acceleration (and metric indexes for `mstats`) for the models or datasets referenced above; otherwise `tstats`/`mstats` may return no results from summaries.


Step 3 — Validate
Compare results with raw Snort or Suricata logs on the sensor, with Cisco Firepower Management Center, or with the IDS or IPS management console event view for the same time range. Confirm that severity and counts line up, then verify field extractions, tags, and index permissions in Splunk.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Stacked area (alerts by severity), Line chart (alert volume trend), Table (top alerts today).
visualization: Stacked area (alerts by severity), Line chart (alert volume trend), Table (top alerts today).
knownFalsePositives: Authorized vulnerability scanners (Tenable, Qualys, Rapid7) and penetration or red team exercises often drive surges in high-severity signature counts. Newly pushed rule sets, noisy campaigns, and SSL inspection can also spike volume without a material incident.
